name: Asking Questions
summary: Form questions in Spanish using question words (qué, dónde, cuándo, cómo, por qué, cuánto, quién) and yes/no intonation.
explanation:
Spanish questions come in two types: yes/no questions and information questions. Yes/no questions are formed simply by changing your intonation — raise your voice at the end. The word order can stay the same as a statement, or you can invert the subject and verb: '¿Tú hablas español?' or '¿Hablas tú español?'

Information questions use question words (interrogatives), which always carry an accent mark: qué (what), dónde (where), cuándo (when), cómo (how), por qué (why), cuánto/a/os/as (how much/many), quién/quiénes (who), cuál/cuáles (which). The question word goes at the beginning of the sentence.

Spanish uses inverted question marks (¿) at the beginning and regular question marks (?) at the end. This helps readers know a question is coming. In informal texting, many people skip the inverted mark, but it's required in formal writing.

examples.en: What's your name? (How do you call yourself?)
How old are you? (How many years do you have?)

commonMistakes: Forgetting accent marks on question words: 'que' (that) vs '¿qué?' (what?) — the accent changes the meaning
Confusing 'por qué' (why — two words) with 'porque' (because — one word)
Using 'do/does' like English: '¿Tú hablas español?' is correct — no auxiliary verb needed
Mixing up 'qué' (what) with 'cuál' (which): '¿Cuál es tu nombre?' is correct for 'What is your name?' (not '¿Qué es tu nombre?')
Forgetting the inverted question mark (¿) at the start
tip: Remember: 'por qué' (why) is two words with accents; 'porque' (because) is one word without an accent. This is one of the most common spelling mistakes even native speakers make.
